metadata.summary: ATAM, ATRAF, Mini-ATAM, TARA, and LAAAM methods for architecture trade-off evaluation with utility trees and quality-attribute scenarios.

content.title: Architecture Trade-off Decision Methods
content.summary: Structured evaluation methods for architecture trade-offs, from the full ATAM workshop (3-4 days, Type 1 irreversible decisions) to lightweight alternatives (Mini-ATAM, TARA, LAAAM) for sprint-level choices. Select method depth based on decision reversibility.

why.p: Trade-off analysis is the systematic process of evaluating competing alternatives in architecture decisions. Every architecture choice involves sacrificing one quality to gain another. The goal is to make informed trade-offs aligned with business priorities. Organizations using structured decision frameworks report 25-35% better outcomes from software investments. Decisions made without analysis cause 67% of failed software implementations (Forrester 2024). Undocumented trade-offs lead teams to revisit settled debates — decision amnesia.

list.item: Major architecture decisions, system redesigns, technology migrations — use full ATAM.
Sprint-level decisions with moderate quality attribute impact — use Mini-ATAM (half day).
Focused quality attribute analysis within a bounded scope — use TARA (2-4 hours).
Iterative architecture evaluation across incremental design sessions — use LAAAM (1 day).
Evaluating reference architectures or architectural frameworks — use ATRAF.
Type-2 reversible decisions (library choice, CSS framework) — analysis paralysis costs more than the wrong choice.
When there is no real choice — only one option meets a hard constraint. Skip the method; document the constraint.
When stakeholders have not been identified yet — agents will invent generic personas and the output looks plausible but binds nobody.
Pure cost questions answered by a spreadsheet (TCO over 3 years with known unit prices).

section.name: method-overview
section.p: ATAM (Architecture Tradeoff Analysis Method) is the industry-standard method developed by SEI/Carnegie Mellon for evaluating architectures against quality attributes. Key outputs: sensitivity points (affect single quality attribute), trade-off points (affect multiple quality attributes), risks and non-risks, risk themes. Typical duration: 3-4 days with full stakeholder participation.

ATRAF (Architecture Tradeoff and Risk Analysis Framework), published May 2025, extends ATAM for evaluating reference architectures and architectural frameworks. Three methods: ATRAM (enhanced risk identification for concrete systems), RATRAM (reference architecture evaluation), AFTRAM (architectural framework evaluation).
Lightweight alternatives by duration and best fit:

list.item: Decision Matrix — 1-2 hours — technology selection, vendor comparison.
Tiny Architectural Review (TARA) — 2-4 hours — focused quality attribute analysis.

section.name: reversibility-classification
section.p: Force reversibility classification (Type 1 vs Type 2) up front. Analysis depth follows from it.

rule.statement: Before applying any decision method, classify the decision as Type 1 (irreversible: database choice, cloud provider, core language, auth provider — use ATAM or deep analysis) or Type 2 (reversible: library choice, API design, caching strategy — use quick analysis and iterate). Analysis depth MUST be proportional to decision reversibility.
rule.rationale: High cost plus irreversible decisions justify 3-4 days of ATAM. Low cost reversible decisions should be made fast and iterated; over-analysis wastes more than the wrong reversible choice would.

example.title: Reversibility check before analysis
example.code: Decision: Choose primary database for new SaaS platform. Reversibility: Type 1 — migrating databases after launch costs 6-12 months of effort. Analysis depth: Full ATAM or at minimum a Decision Matrix with ATAM-style scenario analysis. Stakeholders required: architects, product owner, ops rep, business stakeholder.

example.title: Over-analyzing a reversible decision
example.code: Decision: Choose a Python HTTP client library (httpx vs requests). Reversibility: Type 2 — swap in a day with find/replace. What NOT to do: 3-day ATAM session with stakeholder workshops. What TO do: Quick decision matrix (1 hour), document, ship.

section.name: atam-procedure
section.p: ATAM Phase 0 (Preparation): identify evaluation team (lead evaluator, technical recorder, business analyst, optional external perspective), gather documentation (business requirements, current architecture, quality attribute requirements, constraints), schedule participants (architects, key developers, product owner, ops representative, business stakeholders), prepare materials (architecture presentation, business drivers summary, scenario templates).

ATAM Phase 1 — Partnership and Preparation (Day 1 Morning): present ATAM method to stakeholders (purpose and outputs, expected participation, timeline), present business drivers (business context, key business goals, major constraints, quality attribute priorities).

ATAM Phase 2 — Architecture Presentation (Day 1 Afternoon): present architecture at appropriate level (high-level overview, key architectural decisions, technology choices, integration points), identify architectural approaches (list approaches used, map approaches to quality attributes, note where approaches conflict).

ATAM Phase 3 — Utility Tree Development (Day 2 Morning): build utility tree with quality attributes as branches and concrete scenarios as leaves, each prioritized by importance and difficulty (H/M/L). Focus analysis on (H,H) scenarios.

ATAM Phase 4 — Architectural Analysis (Day 2 Afternoon through Day 3): analyze each high-priority scenario (how does architecture support this, what architectural decisions affect this, what are the risks), identify sensitivity points (components/decisions affecting single quality attribute), identify trade-off points (components/decisions affecting multiple quality attributes), catalog risks and non-risks.

ATAM Phase 5 — Brainstorming and Scenario Prioritization (Day 3): brainstorm additional scenarios with stakeholders (growth, failure, change, exploratory), prioritize through voting (each stakeholder gets votes, consolidate similar scenarios), analyze additional high-priority scenarios.

ATAM Phase 6 — Results Presentation: compile findings (sensitivity points list, trade-off points list, risks and risk themes, non-risks), present to stakeholders (architecture strengths, identified risks and trade-offs, recommendations), document outcomes (full ATAM report, executive summary, action items).

section.name: utility-tree
section.p: The utility tree is the ATAM artifact that structures quality attribute scenarios. Build it as a hierarchy: root Utility, branches for each quality attribute (Performance, Availability, Security, Modifiability, Scalability), leaves as concrete measurable scenarios with (Importance, Difficulty) priority tuples.

rule.statement: Every ATAM scenario MUST include a quantitative response measure (e.g., "API response under 200ms at p95", "recovery in under 15 minutes"). Qualitative measures ("fast", "reliable") are not acceptable — they cannot be used to evaluate whether the architecture satisfies the scenario.
rule.rationale: Agents and humans both default to qualitative measures. Without a number and unit the scenario cannot drive architectural analysis or acceptance testing.

example.title: Well-formed utility tree scenario
example.code: Performance └── Latency └── API response < 200ms for 95th percentile (Importance: H, Difficulty: M) Source: end user Stimulus: normal load request Artifact: API gateway + service Environment: normal operation Response: return data Response Measure: p95 latency < 200ms measured at load balancer

example.title: Scenario missing response measure
example.code: Performance └── Latency └── API should be fast (Importance: H, Difficulty: M) Response Measure: "system is responsive" ← not measurable, useless

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Use sonnet for the bulk of comparison work, opus only on irreversible Type-1 decisions where novel framing matters. Run a two-pass: pass 1 (diverge) generates a wide option set with raw criteria; pass 2 (converge) ranks with weights from the human, then a separate reviewer subagent challenges each row asking "what would make this worse than it looks". Output goes into an ADR plus a stakeholder-rendered summary table.
ATAM relies on stakeholder workshops; an agent cannot run one — it can only seed scenarios. Sensitivity vs trade-off point distinction is lost on most LLMs; they label everything a trade-off.

rule.statement: Avoid letting one agent both generate options and score them. Use separate subagent roles: one for option generation (diverge), one for scoring (converge), one for red-teaming (challenge each winning row with a failure scenario in the next 24 months).
rule.rationale: An agent scores its own ideas higher (selection bias). The faion-brainstorm skill's diverge/converge/review cycle maps directly onto this split.

section.prompt:
Generate quality attribute scenarios for [system description].
Business context: - [Business driver 1] - [Business driver 2]
System characteristics: - [Characteristic 1] - [Characteristic 2]
Generate 10 scenarios across: Performance, Availability, Security, Modifiability, Scalability.
For each scenario: **Scenario [N]: [Name]** - Source: [Who/what triggers this] - Stimulus: [The event] - Artifact: [What is affected] - Environment: [System state] - Response: [What should happen] - Response Measure: [How to verify — must be quantitative with unit] - Priority: (Importance H/M/L, Difficulty H/M/L)
Focus on scenarios that reveal architectural trade-offs.

Analyze this architecture for sensitivity points:
[Architecture description or diagram]
Key quality attributes: - [QA1]: Target [metric] - [QA2]: Target [metric]
For each architectural element/decision: 1. Which quality attributes does it affect? 2. How sensitive is [QA] to changes in this element? 3. What would break if this element fails/degrades?
Identify: single points of failure, bottleneck candidates, high-sensitivity components. Present as a sensitivity matrix and highlight top 5 concerns.

Identify architectural risks for [system/decision]:
Context: [Architecture description] Constraints: [List constraints] Team: [Team size and expertise]
Analyze risks in categories: 1. Technical risks (can we build it?) 2. Operational risks (can we run it?) 3. Integration risks (will it work with X?) 4. Scale risks (will it handle growth?) 5. Security risks (what could go wrong?) 6. Organizational risks (team, skills, politics)
For each risk: description, probability (H/M/L), impact (H/M/L), early warning signs, mitigation options. Group into themes and prioritize top 5 for immediate attention.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: LLMs invent crisp criteria weights that look authoritative but reflect generic priors, not your business context. Weights must come from humans. Agents converge on "balanced" middle options; without forcing extremes the matrix becomes useless. Hidden trade-offs (org politics, vendor lock-in past 3 years, hiring market) rarely surface unless asked explicitly.

antipattern.description: Accepting LLM-generated criteria weights without human validation.
antipattern.reason: LLM weights reflect generic priors, not your team size, budget, regulatory context, or strategic priorities. A weight of 4 for "scalability" may be right for Netflix and wrong for a 10-user internal tool.

antipattern.description: For ATAM scenarios, accepting qualitative response measures ("system is fast").
antipattern.reason: Agents skip the response measure or make it qualitative. Always demand a number with unit. Without it the scenario cannot drive architectural analysis.
